Contrast: Time feasibility — technology feasibility tests whether the solution is technically achievable, while time feasibility tests whether it can be delivered within the needed timeframe.
Example: Before approving a cloud analytics initiative, the team assesses whether existing infrastructure, security controls, and support skills can handle the platform.
Source: PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms — Technology feasibility

Core Meaning: A framework for evaluating an organisation's performance from the perspectives of profit, people, and the planet.
Expansion: PMI defines triple bottom line as a framework for considering the full cost of doing business by evaluating a company's bottom line from the perspective of profit, people, and the planet. It is used to broaden decision making beyond financial return alone and to consider social and environmental effects alongside economic outcomes. The concept appears in PMI's glossary as a formal term.
Contrast: Value — triple bottom line is a sustainability-oriented evaluation framework, while value in PMI refers to the excess of benefits over investment gained from achieving goals.
Example: A project team assessing a new manufacturing site weighs expected profit, worker wellbeing, and environmental impact before recommending a location.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge — triple bottom line, page 274

Core Meaning: A measurable improvement in performance, value, or outcome attributable to a change, initiative, or delivered capability.
Expansion: Uplift is used in practice to describe the positive gain produced by a project outcome, such as better revenue, quality, speed, or customer results. It is usually discussed in benefits and value conversations rather than as a formal PMI glossary term.
Contrast: Benefit — uplift is the measured improvement itself, while a benefit is the broader advantageous outcome created by change.
Example: A digital service project reports a 15 percent conversion uplift after simplifying the customer checkout flow.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: The process of generating tangible or intangible benefits for an organisation and its stakeholders through project work.
Expansion: Value creation is the reason projects are undertaken: to produce outcomes worth more to stakeholders than the resources consumed. It can take many forms - improved efficiency, lower cost, higher revenue, better customer satisfaction, or new market opportunities. Project managers drive it by aligning objectives with strategy, using resources effectively, and encouraging innovation. In PMBOK 7 it sits at the centre of the value delivery system, which reframes projects as components of value delivery rather than isolated efforts to hit scope, cost, and schedule.
Deep Dive:
What it is: value creation is the generation of benefit for the organisation and its stakeholders, whether financial (revenue, savings) or non-financial (capability, satisfaction, reputation, social good). It is measured by the worth of outcomes, not the volume of output produced.

Why it matters: PMBOK 7 moved the profession from a deliverable-centric view to a value-centric one. Projects, programs, and portfolios are seen as parts of a value delivery system that connects work to strategy, so a team is expected to ask not only whether the deliverable is done but whether it enables value.

The common misconception: that finishing the deliverable creates the value. In practice creation only sets up the potential; the value is realised later through adoption, use, and sustained operation, which is why creation and realization are treated as distinct steps.

How it shows up in projects: a team building a new analytics platform creates value not by shipping the software but by enabling faster, better decisions once people rely on it - so scope is shaped around the outcomes that generate that benefit.
Contrast: Value realization - value creation generates the potential benefits, while value realization is the later confirmation that those benefits are actually achieved and sustained.
Example: A retailer's inventory-optimisation project creates value by cutting stockouts and holding costs, raising both sales and margin once the new process is adopted.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Seventh Edition - value delivery system

Core Meaning: Value delivery is the creation of useful outcomes, benefits, and capabilities that satisfy stakeholder needs and advance strategic objectives.
Expansion: PMI treats value delivery as the central purpose of projects and project management rather than defining success only by completing scope on time and on budget. It connects project outputs to outcomes, benefits, and strategic relevance across products, portfolios, programs, and projects. This perspective shifts attention from activity completion to whether the work meaningfully improves the organisation or its stakeholders.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Value delivery is the generation of beneficial results from project work, including capabilities, outcomes, benefits, and strategic contributions. It links execution to the reason the work exists in the first place.

Why it matters and where it applies: organisations invest in projects to create value, not merely to produce deliverables. This applies across predictive, agile, and hybrid environments, and it is especially important in portfolio selection, product development, transformation efforts, and benefits-focused governance.

The most common misconception: that delivering value is the same as finishing all planned scope. A project can complete its deliverables and still fall short of value if the outputs are unused, poorly adopted, mistimed, or misaligned to stakeholder need.

How it shows up in real projects: teams define success in terms of benefits, user outcomes, and strategic fit; they prioritise features and decisions based on expected impact; and they review progress using measures such as adoption, realised benefits, customer experience, or operational improvement.
Contrast: Deliverable — a deliverable is a tangible or verifiable output of work; value delivery is the broader realization of beneficial outcomes from those outputs.
Example: A hospital digitises patient intake forms, but the project measures success not only by deploying the software but by reducing wait times, improving data accuracy, and increasing patient satisfaction.
Source: PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition, Introduction and System for Value Delivery, pp. 1-17

Core Meaning: A system of governance, processes, and activities used to create and sustain value for stakeholders.
Expansion: The value delivery system is the broader organisational system through which portfolios, programs, projects, products, and operations contribute to stakeholder value. PMI uses it to position projects as one part of a larger ecosystem rather than isolated efforts. It helps practitioners connect execution decisions with strategy, governance, and realised outcomes.
Deep Dive:
What it is: The value delivery system is the integrated environment through which an organisation turns ideas, investments, and work into stakeholder value. It links strategy, governance, project work, and ongoing operations.

Why it matters and where it applies: it matters because successful project management depends on more than delivery mechanics inside a single team. It is especially relevant in organisations managing multiple initiatives, where alignment among strategy, funding, execution, and benefits realisation determines whether work actually pays off.

The most common misconception: that the value delivery system is just another name for a project life cycle or PMO framework. It is broader than either, because it covers the full organisational context for creating and sustaining value.

How it shows up in real projects: project teams align scope with strategic goals, governance bodies make investment decisions, portfolios prioritise initiatives, and operating teams absorb outputs so benefits can be sustained after delivery.
Contrast: Project life cycle — A value delivery system is the broader organisational system for creating value; a project life cycle is the sequence of phases within one project.
Example: A hospital network uses strategy reviews, portfolio prioritisation, project delivery, and operational handoff together to ensure a digital records initiative improves patient care and reporting performance.
Source: PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition — Value Delivery System, page citation not confirmed in available source excerpt

Core Meaning: The value of a product or service that an organization communicates to its customers.
Expansion: A value proposition states the benefit an organisation says its product or service will provide to customers. It is outward-facing and helps explain why stakeholders should choose one offering over another. In PMI usage, it is about communicating expected benefit rather than proving realised outcomes.
Contrast: Value — value is the worth actually realised or perceived in context; a value proposition is the communicated promise of that value.
Example: A project to launch a new field-service platform is framed to customers as reducing technician scheduling delays through real-time dispatching.
Source: PMIstandards+ Navigator Glossary, value proposition, page not available in retrieved source

Core Meaning: The process of ensuring the intended benefits and value of a project are actually achieved and sustained over time.
Expansion: Value realization shifts the focus from delivering outputs to confirming that expected value materialises after delivery. It relies on defining value objectives up front, then tracking outcomes against clear metrics and KPIs and adjusting as needed to close any gap. Because value often depends on adoption and use well beyond project closure, realization frequently continues into operations. In PMBOK 7's value delivery framing, this is how a team demonstrates that an investment paid off rather than assuming completion equals success.
Contrast: Value creation - value creation generates the potential benefits, while value realization confirms those benefits are actually attained and maintained after delivery.
Example: After launching a self-service portal, the team keeps monitoring call-deflection and satisfaction metrics for two quarters to verify the projected savings are realised.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Seventh Edition - value delivery system; not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A concise expression of the desired future state that guides and inspires project or product direction.
Expansion: A vision statement describes the future condition a project, product, or initiative is intended to help create. It provides directional clarity for decision-making and helps align stakeholders around purpose rather than only around tasks. In practice, it is most useful when it is short, memorable, and connected to strategic intent.
Contrast: Business case — A vision statement describes the desired future state; a business case justifies the investment and explains why the work should be undertaken.
Example: A city transit project uses the vision statement 'seamless, accessible public transport for every commuter' to guide design tradeoffs across ticketing, signage, and mobile access.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge Seventh Edition — Vision statement, pages 34 and 168

Core Meaning: A matrix organizational structure in which the functional manager has more authority than the project manager.
Expansion: In a weak matrix, the project manager has limited formal authority and often serves mainly as a coordinator or expediter. Functional managers control most resources, priorities, and staff decisions, which can constrain project speed and decision-making. PMI uses the term as one end of the matrix spectrum, contrasted with balanced and strong matrix structures.
Contrast: Strong matrix — a weak matrix leaves authority primarily with functional managers, while a strong matrix gives the project manager substantially greater authority over resources and decisions.
Example: In an IT department with a weak matrix structure, the project manager coordinates a system upgrade but depends on functional managers to assign engineers and approve schedule changes.
Source: PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, organizational structure type weak matrix, exact page not confirmed
